era_context: The Old Kingdom of Egypt (Dynasty VI) is in its final phase. Naram-Sin's Akkad Empire is at its height. The Harappan civilization is in its mature phase. This period represents the apex of Bronze Age civilization in the Old World, though underlying stresses were accumulating.

events.title: Pepi II's reign: late Old Kingdom Egypt
events.description: Pepi II continued to rule Egypt during this year. The Old Kingdom's centralized authority was gradually eroding, with provincial governors accumulating power and wealth. The pharaonic state remained functional, but the mechanisms of centralization were weakening. This period set the stage for the First Intermediate Period.
events.certainty_note: No specific events are recorded for this year. The dating is conventional and carries ±50–100 year uncertainty.

events.title: Naram-Sin's Akkad Empire: territorial apex
events.description: Naram-Sin's reign continued during this year. The Akkad Empire had reached its maximum territorial extent, controlling Mesopotamia, the Levant, and parts of the Zagros Mountains. The empire's administration was sophisticated, but internal resistance and external pressure from the Gutians were building. The empire would collapse within a generation.

sources.name: Akkadian royal inscriptions and administrative texts

events.certainty_note: Naram-Sin's reign is well-attested, but no specific events are recorded for this particular year.

events.title: Harappan civilization: mature phase peak
events.description: The Harappan civilization was at its peak during this year. Major urban centers were fully developed and functioning. The civilization's economy was based on agriculture, craft production, and extensive trade networks. The undeciphered script was in use for administrative and commercial purposes. This period represents the height of Harappan civilization before its gradual decline in the 19th century BCE.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Indus Valley sites

events.certainty_note: The Harappan civilization's existence and chronology are well-established archaeologically.

disconfirming_evidence: No specific events are recorded for this year in any tradition. The entries are generalizations about broader patterns.
historiographic_note: This year is undocumented in the historical record. The entries represent inferences from archaeological and epigraphic evidence for the broader period. Year-by-year precision is not possible for the 3rd millennium BCE outside of a few well-documented reigns.

graph_edges.note: Pepi II's reign and the erosion of Old Kingdom authority led to the First Intermediate Period
Naram-Sin's imperial overreach preceded the Akkad Empire's collapse
